mnemonics: Time-Traveling Transactions: MVCC creates versions of data so transactions can see a consistent snapshot from their start time, preventing conflicts like time-travelers not interfering with each other.
keyPoints: Read operations do not block write operations, and vice-versa, increasing concurrency.
Each transaction works with a snapshot of the database as it existed at the transaction's start time.
It maintains multiple versions of a data item; outdated versions are cleaned up when no active transactions need them.
commonMistakes: Confusing MVCC with locking protocols; it uses versioning, not locks, for read operations.
Forgetting that it can lead to storage overhead due to maintaining multiple versions of data items.
Assuming a transaction always sees the very latest committed data, when it actually sees a snapshot from its start time.
